title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 04

questions.prompt: Originalized West African court account, Mali, c. 1350: “The mansa receives petitions beneath a canopy while learned men recite law and merchants display gold dust sealed in leather pouches. Envoys say that justice in the capital is known from the desert edge to the forest markets.” What was the most likely purpose of this account?

choices.A: To portray Mali’s ruler as a source of justice, wealth, and legitimate authority
choices.B: To argue that Mali had no connection to long-distance commerce
choices.C: To show that merchants replaced the mansa as the head of state
choices.D: To describe an industrial bureaucracy based on factory labor

questions.correctAnswerText: To portray Mali’s ruler as a source of justice, wealth, and legitimate authority
questions.explanation: The account emphasizes petitions, law, gold, envoys, and the ruler’s reputation for justice.

questions.prompt: Originalized West African court account, Mali, c. 1350: “The mansa receives petitions beneath a canopy while learned men recite law and merchants display gold dust sealed in leather pouches. Envoys say that justice in the capital is known from the desert edge to the forest markets.” Which phrase best supports the claim that Islamic learning influenced Mali’s political culture?

choices.A: “Gold dust sealed in leather pouches”
choices.B: “Learned men recite law”
choices.C: “Forest markets”
choices.D: “Beneath a canopy”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Learned men recite law”
questions.explanation: Learned men reciting law points to Islamic legal and scholarly influence at court.

questions.prompt: Originalized West African court account, Mali, c. 1350: “The mansa receives petitions beneath a canopy while learned men recite law and merchants display gold dust sealed in leather pouches. Envoys say that justice in the capital is known from the desert edge to the forest markets.” Which broader economic context best explains the display of gold dust?

choices.A: The collapse of Saharan routes before 1200
choices.B: The rise of plantation slavery in the Atlantic world
choices.C: Mali’s participation in regional and trans-Saharan gold commerce
choices.D: The spread of industrial mining corporations in West Africa

questions.correctAnswerText: Mali’s participation in regional and trans-Saharan gold commerce
questions.explanation: Mali’s wealth was closely tied to gold production and trade across Saharan and regional networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized West African court account, Mali, c. 1350: “The mansa receives petitions beneath a canopy while learned men recite law and merchants display gold dust sealed in leather pouches. Envoys say that justice in the capital is known from the desert edge to the forest markets.” The account most directly suggests which feature of Mali’s society?

choices.A: The absence of urban centers in West Africa
choices.B: The complete separation of religion, trade, and government
choices.C: The replacement of royal authority by nomadic councils
choices.D: The interaction of rulers, scholars, merchants, and regional communities

questions.correctAnswerText: The interaction of rulers, scholars, merchants, and regional communities
questions.explanation: The account links royal justice, Islamic learning, merchants, gold, and regional markets.

questions.prompt: Originalized merchant letter, Red Sea port, c. 1275: “Send pepper through the Aden broker, and have the cloth marked before it leaves the warehouse. The captain waits for safe passage north, and our partner in Cairo will settle the debt by letter rather than coin.” Which development is best reflected in the letter?

choices.A: Long-distance merchants used brokers, credit instruments, and port networks to organize trade.
choices.B: Red Sea commerce ended because merchants rejected credit.
choices.C: Cairo was isolated from Indian Ocean and Mediterranean exchange.
choices.D: Maritime trade required the direct rule of every port by one empire.

questions.correctAnswerText: Long-distance merchants used brokers, credit instruments, and port networks to organize trade.
questions.explanation: The letter mentions a broker, marked cloth, a partner in Cairo, and settlement by letter.

questions.prompt: Originalized merchant letter, Red Sea port, c. 1275: “Send pepper through the Aden broker, and have the cloth marked before it leaves the warehouse. The captain waits for safe passage north, and our partner in Cairo will settle the debt by letter rather than coin.” Which phrase most directly supports the claim that commercial technologies reduced the need to move metal currency?

choices.A: “The captain waits”
choices.B: “The cloth marked”
choices.C: “Through the Aden broker”
choices.D: “Settle the debt by letter rather than coin”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Settle the debt by letter rather than coin”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows use of credit or written commercial instruments instead of metal currency.

questions.prompt: Originalized merchant letter, Red Sea port, c. 1275: “Send pepper through the Aden broker, and have the cloth marked before it leaves the warehouse. The captain waits for safe passage north, and our partner in Cairo will settle the debt by letter rather than coin.” Which audience would most likely have used a document like this?

choices.A: Factory inspectors regulating child labor
choices.B: Merchants coordinating transactions across distant ports
choices.C: Kings claiming divine descent in mountain kingdoms
choices.D: Soldiers mobilized during a twentieth-century total war

questions.correctAnswerText: Merchants coordinating transactions across distant ports
questions.explanation: The details about Aden, Cairo, goods, brokers, and debt settlement are commercial instructions.

questions.prompt: Originalized merchant letter, Red Sea port, c. 1275: “Send pepper through the Aden broker, and have the cloth marked before it leaves the warehouse. The captain waits for safe passage north, and our partner in Cairo will settle the debt by letter rather than coin.” The movement described in the letter was most directly shaped by which geographic factor?

choices.A: The Red Sea’s position linking Indian Ocean goods with Mediterranean and Nile routes
choices.B: The absence of navigable sea routes between Africa and Asia
choices.C: The freezing of Arabian ports for much of the year
choices.D: The isolation of Egypt from Afro-Eurasian exchange

questions.correctAnswerText: The Red Sea’s position linking Indian Ocean goods with Mediterranean and Nile routes
questions.explanation: The Red Sea connected goods from the Indian Ocean world to Egypt and the Mediterranean.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of a Mississippian center, c. 1200: “A large earthen mound rises beside a plaza. Nearby are fields of maize, elite residences, and traces of copper ornaments and marine shells brought from distant regions.” Which interpretation is best supported by the description?

choices.A: The center combined agriculture, social hierarchy, and regional exchange.
choices.B: The center depended on ocean-going merchant fleets.
choices.C: The center was built by European settlers after 1500.
choices.D: The center lacked evidence of organized labor or elite status.

questions.correctAnswerText: The center combined agriculture, social hierarchy, and regional exchange.
questions.explanation: Maize fields, elite residences, mound construction, copper, and shells show agriculture, hierarchy, and exchange.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of a Mississippian center, c. 1200: “A large earthen mound rises beside a plaza. Nearby are fields of maize, elite residences, and traces of copper ornaments and marine shells brought from distant regions.” Which evidence best supports the claim that exchange networks existed beyond the immediate settlement?

choices.A: “Fields of maize”
choices.B: “A large earthen mound”
choices.C: “Marine shells brought from distant regions”
choices.D: “Beside a plaza”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Marine shells brought from distant regions”
questions.explanation: Marine shells at an inland center show exchange beyond the immediate settlement.

questions.prompt: Originalized Safavid royal decree, early seventeenth century: “Let the shrine road be repaired, and let the judges ensure that the Friday sermon names the shah. The tribal cavalry shall serve at the frontier, but tax collectors must send the silk dues to Isfahan without delay.” What was the most likely purpose of this decree?

choices.A: To coordinate religious legitimacy, military service, and revenue collection for the state
choices.B: To invite European parliaments to govern Persia
choices.C: To abolish all trade in silk
choices.D: To end the political role of Shi’a religious institutions

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate religious legitimacy, military service, and revenue collection for the state
questions.explanation: The decree links shrine roads, Friday sermons, cavalry, silk dues, and Isfahan.

questions.prompt: Originalized Safavid royal decree, early seventeenth century: “Let the shrine road be repaired, and let the judges ensure that the Friday sermon names the shah. The tribal cavalry shall serve at the frontier, but tax collectors must send the silk dues to Isfahan without delay.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religion helped legitimize Safavid rule?

choices.A: “Tribal cavalry”
choices.B: “Silk dues”
choices.C: “Friday sermon names the shah”
choices.D: “Without delay”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Friday sermon names the shah”
questions.explanation: Naming the shah in the Friday sermon connected religious practice to political legitimacy.

questions.prompt: Originalized Safavid royal decree, early seventeenth century: “Let the shrine road be repaired, and let the judges ensure that the Friday sermon names the shah. The tribal cavalry shall serve at the frontier, but tax collectors must send the silk dues to Isfahan without delay.” The reference to silk dues most directly reflects which common feature of early modern empires?

choices.A: The disappearance of luxury trade from state finance
choices.B: The reliance of states on commercial revenue and taxation
choices.C: The end of bureaucratic record keeping
choices.D: The replacement of agrarian taxes by industrial income taxes

questions.correctAnswerText: The reliance of states on commercial revenue and taxation
questions.explanation: Silk dues show the use of trade-related revenue in imperial finance.

questions.prompt: Originalized Safavid royal decree, early seventeenth century: “Let the shrine road be repaired, and let the judges ensure that the Friday sermon names the shah. The tribal cavalry shall serve at the frontier, but tax collectors must send the silk dues to Isfahan without delay.” Which social tension is most directly suggested by the decree?

choices.A: Conflict between factory workers and industrial owners
choices.B: Competition between royal centralization and the autonomy of tribal military elites
choices.C: Conflict between abolitionists and enslavers in the Atlantic world
choices.D: Rivalry between Cold War superpowers over nuclear weapons

questions.correctAnswerText: Competition between royal centralization and the autonomy of tribal military elites
questions.explanation: The decree requires tribal cavalry service while directing revenue to the capital, suggesting central control over elite groups.

questions.prompt: Described Benin palace plaque, early modern West Africa: “A bronze plaque shows the oba larger than the attendants beside him. Portuguese traders appear at the edge carrying metal goods, while palace officials hold coral regalia and ceremonial weapons.” Which interpretation is best supported by the plaque description?

choices.A: Benin’s ruler used visual hierarchy and court regalia to display authority.
choices.B: Portuguese traders controlled the oba’s palace.
choices.C: Benin had no contact with overseas commerce.
choices.D: Palace art rejected political symbolism.

questions.correctAnswerText: Benin’s ruler used visual hierarchy and court regalia to display authority.
questions.explanation: The larger oba, palace officials, regalia, and ceremonial weapons display political authority visually.

questions.prompt: Described Benin palace plaque, early modern West Africa: “A bronze plaque shows the oba larger than the attendants beside him. Portuguese traders appear at the edge carrying metal goods, while palace officials hold coral regalia and ceremonial weapons.” Which broader context best explains the presence of Portuguese traders in the image?

choices.A: West African states engaged selectively with Atlantic commercial contacts after 1450.
choices.B: European traders founded Benin as a settler colony.
choices.C: Benin’s economy depended on industrial factories.
choices.D: Portuguese traders ended all local political authority in West Africa.

questions.correctAnswerText: West African states engaged selectively with Atlantic commercial contacts after 1450.
questions.explanation: Portuguese traders at the edge of the plaque show external contact without erasing Benin’s court authority.

questions.prompt: Described Benin palace plaque, early modern West Africa: “A bronze plaque shows the oba larger than the attendants beside him. Portuguese traders appear at the edge carrying metal goods, while palace officials hold coral regalia and ceremonial weapons.” Which argument is best supported by the plaque?

choices.A: Early modern African states lacked artistic traditions.
choices.B: Benin’s court used material culture to present political authority while engaging foreign trade.
choices.C: European merchants replaced African rulers wherever they appeared.
choices.D: Atlantic trade immediately eliminated African court culture.

questions.correctAnswerText: Benin’s court used material culture to present political authority while engaging foreign trade.
questions.explanation: The plaque combines court symbolism, regalia, hierarchy, and Portuguese commercial presence.

questions.prompt: Originalized report from a Spanish viceroy in Lima, c. 1600: “Local officials must record tribute villages carefully, for false counts reduce the king’s revenue. Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors if they provide laborers for roads and town works. Priests request more interpreters for confession and instruction.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To explain how colonial officials organized tribute, labor, and religious administration
choices.B: To abolish all Indigenous offices in the Andes
choices.C: To announce the end of Spanish rule in South America
choices.D: To promote equal citizenship for all colonial subjects

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how colonial officials organized tribute, labor, and religious administration
questions.explanation: The report discusses tribute counts, laborers, Native nobles, priests, and interpreters.

questions.prompt: Originalized report from a Spanish viceroy in Lima, c. 1600: “Local officials must record tribute villages carefully, for false counts reduce the king’s revenue. Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors if they provide laborers for roads and town works. Priests request more interpreters for confession and instruction.” Which phrase best supports the claim that Spanish authorities incorporated some Indigenous elites into colonial rule?

choices.A: “False counts reduce the king’s revenue”
choices.B: “Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors”
choices.C: “Priests request more interpreters”
choices.D: “Roads and town works”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows Spanish incorporation of some Indigenous elites into colonial administration.

questions.prompt: Originalized report from a Spanish viceroy in Lima, c. 1600: “Local officials must record tribute villages carefully, for false counts reduce the king’s revenue. Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors if they provide laborers for roads and town works. Priests request more interpreters for confession and instruction.” Which broader process best explains the priests’ request?

choices.A: The spread of Protestantism through South America
choices.B: The use of Christian missionary activity to reshape Indigenous religious life
choices.C: The end of Catholic influence in Spanish colonies
choices.D: The replacement of missionary work by industrial education

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of Christian missionary activity to reshape Indigenous religious life
questions.explanation: Priests needing interpreters for confession and instruction reflects missionary conversion efforts.

questions.prompt: Originalized report from a Spanish viceroy in Lima, c. 1600: “Local officials must record tribute villages carefully, for false counts reduce the king’s revenue. Native nobles who collect tribute shall keep their honors if they provide laborers for roads and town works. Priests request more interpreters for confession and instruction.” Which statement best evaluates the colonial system described?

choices.A: It relied only on voluntary contracts between equal citizens.
choices.B: It combined imperial revenue extraction with adapted local hierarchies and missionary goals.
choices.C: It ended the use of tribute in Spanish America.
choices.D: It separated economic administration entirely from political rule.

questions.correctAnswerText: It combined imperial revenue extraction with adapted local hierarchies and missionary goals.
questions.explanation: The report links tribute, labor, Native nobles, priests, and interpreters.

questions.prompt:
Table: Atlantic slave-trade records for one West Central African port, simplified pattern
Period | Major destination | Recorded pattern
1670s | Brazil | Mostly young adult men shipped for plantation and mining labor
1720s | Caribbean | Larger shipments during sugar expansion
1780s | Multiple American ports | Continued forced migration despite abolitionist criticism
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: The forced movement of enslaved Africans was tied to labor demands in American plantation and mining economies.
choices.B: The Atlantic slave trade ended before sugar plantation expansion.
choices.C: Enslaved Africans migrated mainly as independent merchants.
choices.D: West Central Africa was isolated from Atlantic commerce.

questions.correctAnswerText: The forced movement of enslaved Africans was tied to labor demands in American plantation and mining economies.
questions.explanation: The table links forced shipments to plantation, mining, sugar, and American destinations.

questions.prompt:
Table: Atlantic slave-trade records for one West Central African port, simplified pattern
Period | Major destination | Recorded pattern
1670s | Brazil | Mostly young adult men shipped for plantation and mining labor
1720s | Caribbean | Larger shipments during sugar expansion
1780s | Multiple American ports | Continued forced migration despite abolitionist criticism
Which evidence best supports the claim that the slave trade disrupted African societies demographically?

choices.A: “Abolitionist criticism”
choices.B: “Multiple American ports”
choices.C: “Mostly young adult men shipped”
choices.D: “Sugar expansion”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Mostly young adult men shipped”
questions.explanation: The forced removal of many young adult men would have major demographic effects.

questions.prompt:
Table: Atlantic slave-trade records for one West Central African port, simplified pattern
Period | Major destination | Recorded pattern
1670s | Brazil | Mostly young adult men shipped for plantation and mining labor
1720s | Caribbean | Larger shipments during sugar expansion
1780s | Multiple American ports | Continued forced migration despite abolitionist criticism
Which broader political consequence was most associated with the trade pattern shown?

choices.A: The complete disappearance of warfare in West Central Africa
choices.B: The weakening of all European maritime empires
choices.C: The separation of Atlantic commerce from state power
choices.D: The growth of African and European states that profited from slave raiding, taxation, or commercial regulation

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of African and European states that profited from slave raiding, taxation, or commercial regulation
questions.explanation: The Atlantic slave trade shaped political power by enriching states and elites involved in capture, taxation, or trade.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing reform essay, c. 1870: “The steamship arrives whether we debate or not. To preserve the dynasty, arsenals must cast modern guns, schools must translate foreign science, and officials must learn that machines can serve moral order rather than destroy it.” Which purpose is most evident in the essay?

choices.A: To argue that selective adoption of foreign technology could strengthen the state
choices.B: To demand the abolition of all Confucian learning
choices.C: To promote immediate republican revolution
choices.D: To reject all military and educational reform

questions.correctAnswerText: To argue that selective adoption of foreign technology could strengthen the state
questions.explanation: The essay supports arsenals, foreign science, and modern machines while aiming to preserve the dynasty.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing reform essay, c. 1870: “The steamship arrives whether we debate or not. To preserve the dynasty, arsenals must cast modern guns, schools must translate foreign science, and officials must learn that machines can serve moral order rather than destroy it.” Which broader context best explains the essay?

choices.A: The absence of European and Japanese pressure on Qing China
choices.B: The collapse of all Asian states before industrialization
choices.C: Qing attempts to respond to military defeats, foreign pressure, and technological gaps
choices.D: The spread of Cold War alliances in East Asia

questions.correctAnswerText: Qing attempts to respond to military defeats, foreign pressure, and technological gaps
questions.explanation: The reform essay fits the Qing effort to strengthen the dynasty after foreign military and technological challenges.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing reform essay, c. 1870: “The steamship arrives whether we debate or not. To preserve the dynasty, arsenals must cast modern guns, schools must translate foreign science, and officials must learn that machines can serve moral order rather than destroy it.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the author wanted reform without fully rejecting existing values?

choices.A: “Steamship arrives”
choices.B: “Foreign science”
choices.C: “Machines can serve moral order”
choices.D: “Arsenals must cast modern guns”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Machines can serve moral order”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows the author wants technology to support existing moral values rather than replace them.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing reform essay, c. 1870: “The steamship arrives whether we debate or not. To preserve the dynasty, arsenals must cast modern guns, schools must translate foreign science, and officials must learn that machines can serve moral order rather than destroy it.” Which statement best evaluates the author’s argument?

choices.A: The author rejects technology because it is foreign.
choices.B: The author claims that technological and educational reform can preserve, rather than overturn, the dynasty.
choices.C: The author argues that industrialization is irrelevant to military power.
choices.D: The author claims that all political legitimacy must come from elected parliaments.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author claims that technological and educational reform can preserve, rather than overturn, the dynasty.
questions.explanation: The author argues that arsenals, science, and machines can help preserve Qing rule.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mexican liberal reform statement, c. 1857: “Citizenship cannot grow where corporate privileges place clergy, army, and village lands outside equal law. The republic must make law common, property transferable, and office open to merit.” Which development is most directly reflected in the statement?

choices.A: Liberal reform efforts to reduce corporate privileges and strengthen republican citizenship
choices.B: A defense of absolute monarchy in Mexico
choices.C: The restoration of encomienda labor after independence
choices.D: The rejection of private property by communist revolutionaries

questions.correctAnswerText: Liberal reform efforts to reduce corporate privileges and strengthen republican citizenship
questions.explanation: The statement criticizes corporate privileges and promotes common law, property reform, and merit.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mexican liberal reform statement, c. 1857: “Citizenship cannot grow where corporate privileges place clergy, army, and village lands outside equal law. The republic must make law common, property transferable, and office open to merit.” Which point of view most likely shaped the statement?

choices.A: A conservative defense of clerical and military privilege
choices.B: A liberal belief that legal equality and property reform would create a modern republic
choices.C: A colonial official’s support for tribute labor
choices.D: A fascist argument for one-party rule

questions.correctAnswerText: A liberal belief that legal equality and property reform would create a modern republic
questions.explanation: The statement emphasizes citizenship, equal law, transferable property, and merit.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mexican liberal reform statement, c. 1857: “Citizenship cannot grow where corporate privileges place clergy, army, and village lands outside equal law. The republic must make law common, property transferable, and office open to merit.” Which phrase most directly supports the claim that the reformers wanted to transform property relations?

choices.A: “Property transferable”
choices.B: “Office open”
choices.C: “Citizenship cannot grow”
choices.D: “Equal law”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Property transferable”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly supports the claim that reformers wanted to change property relations.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mexican liberal reform statement, c. 1857: “Citizenship cannot grow where corporate privileges place clergy, army, and village lands outside equal law. The republic must make law common, property transferable, and office open to merit.” Which broader ideological trend most directly influenced the statement?

choices.A: Conservative restoration of hereditary privilege
choices.B: Liberal nationalism emphasizing citizenship, constitutions, and equality before the law
choices.C: Social Darwinism used to justify imperial conquest
choices.D: Nonalignment during the Cold War

questions.correctAnswerText: Liberal nationalism emphasizing citizenship, constitutions, and equality before the law
questions.explanation: The statement reflects liberal ideas about citizenship, republicanism, legal equality, and merit.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of French Algeria, c. 1880–1910, shows European settler vineyards expanding near coastal cities, rail lines carrying wine and grain to ports, and shaded zones marking communal lands transferred from Algerian communities to colonial owners.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Settler colonialism reshaped land use and connected colonial agriculture to export markets.
choices.B: Algerian communities gained full control of coastal railroads.
choices.C: French Algeria avoided integration into global trade.
choices.D: Colonial railways were built only for pilgrimage travel.

questions.correctAnswerText: Settler colonialism reshaped land use and connected colonial agriculture to export markets.
questions.explanation: The map shows vineyards, land transfers, rail lines, and exports through ports.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of French Algeria, c. 1880–1910, shows European settler vineyards expanding near coastal cities, rail lines carrying wine and grain to ports, and shaded zones marking communal lands transferred from Algerian communities to colonial owners.” The pattern shown most directly reflects which process?

choices.A: The decline of export agriculture under imperial rule
choices.B: The end of European migration to North Africa
choices.C: The use of colonial infrastructure and land policies to support settler economies
choices.D: The replacement of colonial production by subsistence farming

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of colonial infrastructure and land policies to support settler economies
questions.explanation: Railways, ports, vineyard expansion, and land transfer show infrastructure and policy supporting settlers.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of French Algeria, c. 1880–1910, shows European settler vineyards expanding near coastal cities, rail lines carrying wine and grain to ports, and shaded zones marking communal lands transferred from Algerian communities to colonial owners.” Which map feature most directly shows the social impact of settler colonialism?

choices.A: The location of coastal cities
choices.B: The rail lines to ports
choices.C: The shaded zones of communal land transfer
choices.D: The labels for wine and grain

questions.correctAnswerText: The shaded zones of communal land transfer
questions.explanation: These areas directly show Algerian communities losing land to colonial owners.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of French Algeria, c. 1880–1910, shows European settler vineyards expanding near coastal cities, rail lines carrying wine and grain to ports, and shaded zones marking communal lands transferred from Algerian communities to colonial owners.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Colonial states could use law, settlement, and transportation to reorganize conquered territories.
choices.B: Settler colonialism always protected Indigenous communal landholding.
choices.C: French rule in Algeria avoided involvement in agriculture.
choices.D: Railroads made imperial land policies unnecessary.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial states could use law, settlement, and transportation to reorganize conquered territories.
questions.explanation: The map links land transfer, settler agriculture, railways, and export ports.

questions.prompt:
Table: Eastern Mediterranean migration, approximate pattern
Period | Common destinations | Common motives recorded in letters
1880s | Egypt and western Africa | Trade, craft work, debt repayment
1900s | Argentina and Brazil | Wages, land opportunities, family networks
1920s | North America and Australia | Chain migration and education
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Migration from the eastern Mediterranean was shaped by economic opportunity and family networks across several regions.
choices.B: Industrial-era migration moved only from Europe to North America.
choices.C: Migrants avoided destinations in Latin America and Africa.
choices.D: Long-distance migration ended before 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: Migration from the eastern Mediterranean was shaped by economic opportunity and family networks across several regions.
questions.explanation: The table shows destinations across Africa, Latin America, North America, and Australia, with motives including wages and family networks.

questions.prompt:
Table: Eastern Mediterranean migration, approximate pattern
Period | Common destinations | Common motives recorded in letters
1880s | Egypt and western Africa | Trade, craft work, debt repayment
1900s | Argentina and Brazil | Wages, land opportunities, family networks
1920s | North America and Australia | Chain migration and education
Which evidence best supports the claim that migration was often sustained by social networks?

choices.A: “Debt repayment”
choices.B: “Argentina and Brazil”
choices.C: “Chain migration and education”
choices.D: “Trade, craft work”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Chain migration and education”
questions.explanation: Chain migration directly indicates migration supported by existing social and family networks.

questions.prompt:
Table: Eastern Mediterranean migration, approximate pattern
Period | Common destinations | Common motives recorded in letters
1880s | Egypt and western Africa | Trade, craft work, debt repayment
1900s | Argentina and Brazil | Wages, land opportunities, family networks
1920s | North America and Australia | Chain migration and education
Which broader context best explains the pattern shown?

choices.A: The isolation of migrants from global transportation systems
choices.B: The complete end of diaspora communities after 1800
choices.C: Steamship travel, expanding labor markets, and migrant communication networks
choices.D: The disappearance of wage labor in the Atlantic world

questions.correctAnswerText: Steamship travel, expanding labor markets, and migrant communication networks
questions.explanation: These factors made long-distance migration more possible and sustained migrant communities.

questions.prompt:
Table: Eastern Mediterranean migration, approximate pattern
Period | Common destinations | Common motives recorded in letters
1880s | Egypt and western Africa | Trade, craft work, debt repayment
1900s | Argentina and Brazil | Wages, land opportunities, family networks
1920s | North America and Australia | Chain migration and education
Which political issue most commonly accompanied migration patterns like those shown?

choices.A: The end of citizenship laws in receiving societies
choices.B: Debates over immigration restrictions, legal status, and assimilation
choices.C: The abolition of all passports and border controls
choices.D: The disappearance of ethnic communities abroad

questions.correctAnswerText: Debates over immigration restrictions, legal status, and assimilation
questions.explanation: Industrial-era migration often prompted receiving states to debate entry restrictions, legal status, and assimilation.

questions.prompt: Originalized diary entry, Chinese city under Japanese occupation, 1938: “The school is closed, the hospital full, and families sleep in alleys after the bombing. Officials speak of empire and order, but soldiers take grain and carts for the army.” What was the most likely purpose of preserving such a diary entry?

choices.A: To record civilian experiences of occupation and wartime violence
choices.B: To promote tourism in occupied cities
choices.C: To praise total war as beneficial to civilians
choices.D: To argue that aerial warfare had no social effects

questions.correctAnswerText: To record civilian experiences of occupation and wartime violence
questions.explanation: The diary describes school closure, hospital crowding, homelessness, bombing, requisitioning, and occupation.

questions.prompt: Originalized diary entry, Chinese city under Japanese occupation, 1938: “The school is closed, the hospital full, and families sleep in alleys after the bombing. Officials speak of empire and order, but soldiers take grain and carts for the army.” Which phrase best supports the claim that technology intensified civilian suffering?

choices.A: “Officials speak”
choices.B: “After the bombing”
choices.C: “Take grain and carts”
choices.D: “Empire and order”

questions.correctAnswerText: “After the bombing”
questions.explanation: Bombing shows how modern military technology intensified harm to civilians.

questions.prompt: Originalized diary entry, Chinese city under Japanese occupation, 1938: “The school is closed, the hospital full, and families sleep in alleys after the bombing. Officials speak of empire and order, but soldiers take grain and carts for the army.” Which statement best evaluates the diary’s historical value?

choices.A: It is valuable because it shows how civilians experienced occupation, requisitioning, and bombing.
choices.B: It is useless because personal accounts cannot reveal wartime conditions.
choices.C: It proves that occupied populations always supported imperial armies.
choices.D: It shows only the viewpoint of military commanders.

questions.correctAnswerText: It is valuable because it shows how civilians experienced occupation, requisitioning, and bombing.
questions.explanation: The diary gives civilian evidence of wartime social disruption and military occupation.

questions.prompt:
Table: Export commodity prices during the Great Depression, simplified index, 1928 = 100
Commodity and region | 1932 index
Tin, Malaya | 45
Nitrates, Chile | 38
Cocoa, Gold Coast | 52
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Export economies in several regions were harmed by falling global commodity prices.
choices.B: The Great Depression affected only industrial Europe and North America.
choices.C: Commodity prices rose steadily during the early 1930s.
choices.D: Colonized regions avoided economic disruption during the depression.

questions.correctAnswerText: Export economies in several regions were harmed by falling global commodity prices.
questions.explanation: Tin, nitrates, and cocoa all fell sharply from the 1928 index of 100.

questions.prompt:
Table: Export commodity prices during the Great Depression, simplified index, 1928 = 100
Commodity and region | 1932 index
Tin, Malaya | 45
Nitrates, Chile | 38
Cocoa, Gold Coast | 52
Which political effect most directly followed from economic disruption like that shown in the table?

choices.A: The permanent end of labor protest in export economies
choices.B: Greater confidence in colonial and liberal economic management everywhere
choices.C: Increased criticism of imperial rule, export dependence, and existing political systems
choices.D: The disappearance of nationalist movements in the Global South

questions.correctAnswerText: Increased criticism of imperial rule, export dependence, and existing political systems
questions.explanation: Depression-era commodity collapses intensified criticism of export dependence and colonial or liberal economic systems.

questions.prompt: Described Cuban literacy campaign poster, 1961: “A young volunteer holds a lantern and a primer while rural adults sit outside a house at night. The slogan reads, ‘To read is to be free and to build the revolution.’ A small national flag appears beside a schoolbook.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Revolutionary governments could use education campaigns to promote political identity and social mobilization.
choices.B: The Cuban revolution rejected mass education.
choices.C: Literacy campaigns were unrelated to political ideology.
choices.D: Rural adults were excluded from postrevolutionary programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Revolutionary governments could use education campaigns to promote political identity and social mobilization.
questions.explanation: The poster links literacy, revolution, rural education, volunteers, and national identity.

questions.prompt: Described Cuban literacy campaign poster, 1961: “A young volunteer holds a lantern and a primer while rural adults sit outside a house at night. The slogan reads, ‘To read is to be free and to build the revolution.’ A small national flag appears beside a schoolbook.” Which detail most directly supports the claim that the campaign sought broad social participation?

choices.A: The small national flag
choices.B: The rural adults studying outside a house
choices.C: The poster’s date
choices.D: The presence of a slogan

questions.correctAnswerText: The rural adults studying outside a house
questions.explanation: This detail shows the campaign reaching adults in rural settings.

questions.prompt: Described Cuban literacy campaign poster, 1961: “A young volunteer holds a lantern and a primer while rural adults sit outside a house at night. The slogan reads, ‘To read is to be free and to build the revolution.’ A small national flag appears beside a schoolbook.” Which broader context best explains the poster?

choices.A: The use of social programs by revolutionary states during Cold War ideological competition
choices.B: The collapse of all socialist movements before World War II
choices.C: The spread of nineteenth-century settler colonialism in Oceania
choices.D: The decline of national identity in postrevolutionary societies

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of social programs by revolutionary states during Cold War ideological competition
questions.explanation: The Cuban literacy campaign was tied to revolutionary social mobilization during the Cold War.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Southeast Asia, 1945–1965, marks the Dutch return to Indonesia after World War II, Indonesian republican centers in Java and Sumatra, diplomatic negotiations at The Hague, and later recognition of Indonesian sovereignty.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Indonesia’s independence struggle combined armed resistance, republican organization, and international diplomacy.
choices.B: Dutch colonial authority ended without conflict or negotiation.
choices.C: Indonesia became independent before World War II began.
choices.D: Decolonization in Southeast Asia was unrelated to the collapse of Japanese occupation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Indonesia’s independence struggle combined armed resistance, republican organization, and international diplomacy.
questions.explanation: The map includes Dutch return, republican centers, negotiations, and recognition of sovereignty.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Southeast Asia, 1945–1965, marks the Dutch return to Indonesia after World War II, Indonesian republican centers in Java and Sumatra, diplomatic negotiations at The Hague, and later recognition of Indonesian sovereignty.” The struggle shown most directly reflects which broader postwar process?

choices.A: The strengthening of all European colonial empires after 1945
choices.B: The expansion of anticolonial nationalism and the creation of new sovereign states
choices.C: The disappearance of diplomacy from independence movements
choices.D: The replacement of nationalism by mercantilist trading companies

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of anticolonial nationalism and the creation of new sovereign states
questions.explanation: Indonesia’s independence reflected the broader post-1945 wave of decolonization.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global climate indicators, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1960 | c. 2020
Atmospheric CO₂ | About 317 ppm | More than 410 ppm
Global mean temperature anomaly | Near 0.0 °C | About +1.2 °C
Annual climate-related disaster reports | Lower | Higher
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Environmental change became a major global issue linked to industrial emissions and climate impacts.
choices.B: Atmospheric CO₂ declined after 1960.
choices.C: Climate-related disasters disappeared by 2020.
choices.D: Global environmental change affected only one region.

questions.correctAnswerText: Environmental change became a major global issue linked to industrial emissions and climate impacts.
questions.explanation: Rising CO₂, temperature anomaly, and disaster reports indicate global climate change.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a Pacific island delegate at an international climate conference, c. 2015: “Our islands did not build the factories that filled the sky, yet the sea reaches our gardens and graves. We ask not for charity, but for justice, adaptation funds, and a future in our own homeland.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To deny that climate change had political consequences
choices.B: To argue for international responsibility and support for vulnerable states
choices.C: To demand a return to nineteenth-century imperial rule
choices.D: To oppose all environmental agreements

questions.correctAnswerText: To argue for international responsibility and support for vulnerable states
questions.explanation: The statement asks for justice, adaptation funds, and protection for vulnerable island communities.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a Pacific island delegate at an international climate conference, c. 2015: “Our islands did not build the factories that filled the sky, yet the sea reaches our gardens and graves. We ask not for charity, but for justice, adaptation funds, and a future in our own homeland.” Which phrase best supports the claim that climate change raised questions of global inequality?

choices.A: “International climate conference”
choices.B: “The sea reaches our gardens”
choices.C: “Did not build the factories that filled the sky”
choices.D: “Future in our own homeland”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Did not build the factories that filled the sky”
questions.explanation: This phrase highlights unequal responsibility for emissions and unequal vulnerability.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a Pacific island delegate at an international climate conference, c. 2015: “Our islands did not build the factories that filled the sky, yet the sea reaches our gardens and graves. We ask not for charity, but for justice, adaptation funds, and a future in our own homeland.” The statement most directly connects present environmental problems to which earlier development?

choices.A: The spread of caravanserais across Central Asia
choices.B: The rise of fossil-fuel-based industrialization and mass production
choices.C: The decline of print culture in reform movements
choices.D: The formation of early agrarian river-valley states

questions.correctAnswerText: The rise of fossil-fuel-based industrialization and mass production
questions.explanation: The delegate connects climate change to factories and industrial emissions.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a Pacific island delegate at an international climate conference, c. 2015: “Our islands did not build the factories that filled the sky, yet the sea reaches our gardens and graves. We ask not for charity, but for justice, adaptation funds, and a future in our own homeland.” Which statement best evaluates the delegate’s argument?

choices.A: The delegate argues that environmental problems should be solved only by private companies.
choices.B: The delegate rejects international cooperation as unnecessary.
choices.C: The delegate frames climate change as both an environmental crisis and a justice issue involving unequal responsibility.
choices.D: The delegate claims that sea-level rise has no connection to industrial history.

questions.correctAnswerText: The delegate frames climate change as both an environmental crisis and a justice issue involving unequal responsibility.
questions.explanation: The statement links sea-level impacts, industrial emissions, justice, and adaptation funding.
